verifyCommand: uv run --extra vision pytest && uv run ruff check .
acceptanceCriteria: Verification passes (uv run --extra vision pytest && uv run ruff check .)
All pre-existing tests still pass unchanged
notes: Post-review hardening for relief-probe (see NEXT_STEPS 'Hardening / rigor backlog'). Two CODE features only — both fully TDD-verifiable on SYNTHETIC/seeded warehouses (never the real data/ warehouse, which is LOCKED by a concurrent full-slice ingest running in the background — do NOT run `relief-probe` CLI against data/relief_probe.duckdb; rely on unit tests with tmp_path). CRITICAL GUARDRAIL: do NOT hand-write or invent any benchmark/lift NUMBERS into README or docs — real numbers are regenerated by a human on the full dataset after the ingest finishes. Qualitative honesty text is fine. Entity key is loan_number (str). Reuse existing modules. ruff line-length 90; typed + documented to match the codebase. Commit one feature per iteration. Read plans/guardrails.md every iteration.

features.title: Baseline comparison in the benchmark (composite vs trivial vs raw-amount)
acceptanceCriteria: benchmark/core.py: add baseline_rankings(con) -> dict[str, list[str]] returning whole-population ranked loan_number lists for 'amount_per_job' (ORDER BY current_approval_amount/jobs_reported DESC, jobs>=1 and amount>0) and 'raw_amount' (ORDER BY current_approval_amount DESC)

run_benchmark(...) result gains a 'baselines' key: {name: {'metrics': ranking_metrics(ranked, positives, base_rate, ks)}} for each baseline, computed against the SAME positives/base_rate/ks as the composite overall
CLI `benchmark` prints a comparison table: composite vs amount_per_job vs raw_amount lift@k (so a reader sees whether the detector machinery beats a one-line sort)

tests/test_benchmark.py extended: on a seeded warehouse, baseline_rankings returns both named rankings with correct ordering (highest $/job first; largest loan first); run_benchmark output includes 'baselines' with computed lift dicts of the documented shape; a planted high-$/job labeled loan appears at/near the top of the amount_per_job baseline
NO benchmark numbers written into README/docs (numbers come from a real run post-ingest); code + tests only
features.notes: Baselines rank the FULL population (unlike composite, which is flagged-only) — that contrast is the point. Reuse ranking_metrics + labeled_fraud_loans. Keep it pure SQL + Python.

features.title: Honest framing for the vision layer (synthetic = plumbing, not validated capability)
acceptanceCriteria: vision/__init__.py exposes a SYNTHETIC_NOTE constant stating clearly: the ELA detector is demonstrated on SYNTHETIC spliced images (a plumbing/methodology demo), NOT validated on real document forgeries; high synthetic accuracy does not imply real-world performance; real anchors (IDNet, Find-it-again) are wired but not run
CLI `vision-demo` prints SYNTHETIC_NOTE; CLI `vision-score` output notes the model is synthetic-trained unless trained on real data
app/dashboard.py Document-authenticity tab surfaces the same caveat near the score
README vision/status wording made honest (qualitative): frame the demo as synthetic plumbing, drop any unqualified accuracy/P(forged) bragging; no fabricated metrics
tests/test_vision.py extended (uses the vision extra): SYNTHETIC_NOTE exists, is non-empty, mentions 'synthetic'; `vision-demo` output contains it (typer.testing.CliRunner)
features.notes: Small, mostly framing. The point of the review: ELA on engineered q25 splices is trivially separable, so the demo proves wiring, not document-fraud detection. Say so plainly.
